results.final_answer:
Yes, the concept of moral law and duty is present in European works within the corpus, though the specific philosophical underpinnings and expressions may differ from the Sanskrit *smṛtis*.

One prominent example is found in **Johann Gottlieb Fichte's *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*** (Reclamation of the Freedom of Thought from the Princes of Europe), specifically in Chapter 2. Fichte discusses "moral law" in the context of individual rights and freedom of thought. He states:

*   "...I may also choose not to avail myself of this permission of the moral law; then I do not exercise my right; I give it up." (work: `johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde`, chapter: 2, paragraph: `p-c72eae`)
*   "...this law first tames our selfishness, which seeks to govern the moral law. Free and disinterested love for theoretical truth, because it is truth, is the..." (work: `johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde`, chapter: 2, paragraph: `p-e62dae`)
*   "...If we have a right to everything that the moral law does not forbid, who could point to a prohibition of the moral law against comm..." (work: `johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde`, chapter: 2, paragraph: `p-3c8e3d`)

These excerpts suggest that Fichte views moral law as a framework that defines permissible actions and guides individual conduct, even in the absence of external governmental intervention. It also implies a personal responsibility to adhere to this moral law, and that it can even "tame our selfishness."

Another work that touches upon "moral laws" is **Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation: VOL I***. In Chapter 18 and 20, Comte discusses how "moral laws" arise from societal forces and customs:

*   "...nd without the intervention of governments, these forces are given the name of *moral laws*, or more simply of *morality*; thus, to violate the laws of morality is to gi..." (work: `charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2`, chapter: 18, paragraph: `p-06a027`)
*   "...regulated by the forces of the first class forms the customs of a people, or its moral laws; all that is regulated by the combined forces of the first and second class fo..." (work: `charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2`, chapter: 20, paragraph: `p-e4a7c9`)

Comte's perspective suggests that moral laws are deeply intertwined with societal norms and customs, and that their influence can extend beyond the reach of public authority.

While the term "dharma" in Sanskrit *smṛtis* encompasses a broader meaning of sacred law, righteousness, and duty, these European works demonstrate a clear engagement with the concepts of moral law and individual duty, albeit from different philosophical standpoints. Fichte emphasizes individual freedom within the bounds of moral law, while Comte highlights the societal origins and influence of moral laws.

results.final_answer: Hwæt! We gefrunan on fyrndagum twelfe under tunglum tireadige hæleð, þeodnes þegnas. No hira þrym alæg camprædenne þonne cumbol hneotan, syððan hie gedældon, swa him dryhten sylf, heofona heahcyning, hlyt getæhte. þæt wæron mære men ofer eorðan, frome folctogan ond fyrdhwate, rofe rincas, þonne rond ond hand on herefelda helm ealgodon, on meotudwange. Wæs hira Matheus sum, se mid Iudeum ongan godspell ærest wordum writan wundorcræfte. þam halig god hlyt geteode ut on þæt igland þær ænig þa git ellþeodigra eðles ne mihte blædes brucan. Oft him bonena hand on herefelda hearde gesceode. Eal wæs þæt mearcland morðre bewunden, feondes facne, folcstede gumena, hæleða eðel. Næs þær hlafes wist werum on þam wonge, ne wæteres drync to bruconne, ah hie blod ond fel, fira flæschoman, feorrancumenra, ðegon geond þa þeode. Swelc wæs þeaw hira þæt hie æghwylcne ellðeodigra dydan him to mose meteþearfendum, þara þe þæt ealand utan sohte. Swylc wæs þæs folces freoðoleas tacen, unlædra eafoð, þæt hie eagena gesihð, hettend heorogrimme, heafodgimmas agetton gealgmode gara ordum. Syððan him geblendan bitere tosomne, dryas þurh dwolcræft, drync unheorne, se onwende gewit, wera ingeþanc, heortan on hreðre, (hyge wæs oncyrred), þæt hie ne murndan æfter mandreame, hæleþ heorogrædige, ac hie hig ond gærs for meteleaste meðe gedrehte. þa wæs Matheus to þære mæran byrig cumen in þa ceastre. þær wæs cirm micel geond Mermedonia, manfulra hloð, fordenera gedræg, syþþan deofles þegnas geascodon æðelinges sið. Eodon him þa togenes, garum gehyrsted, lungre under linde, (nalas late wæron), eorre æscberend, to þam orlege. Hie þam halgan þær handa gebundon ond fæstnodon feondes cræfte, hæleð hellfuse, ond his heafdes segl abreoton mid billes ecge. Hwæðre he in breostum þa git herede in heortan heofonrices weard, þeah ðe he atres drync atulne onfenge. Eadig ond onmod, he mid elne forð wyrðode wordum wuldres aldor, heofonrices weard, halgan stefne, of carcerne. Him wæs Cristes lof on fyrhðlocan fæste bewunden.
